APTHOUS SMC STOMATITIS
It is a common disease characterised by development of or painful, recurrant,
solitary or multiple ulcerations of the oral mucasa where the ulcers heal
spontaneously and there are no signs of any other disease.
ETIOLOGY
It has been considered as an idiopathic disease and is associated with
many predispasing factors

CLASSIFICATION
ACCORDING TO MORPHOLOGY
1> Minor Apthae - also called as canker sores in which the ulcers are
oval shaped, less than 1 cm in diameter, and heal
without scar
2> Major Apthae - also called as Sutton's disease or Periadenitis
mucasa necrotica recurrens and the ulcers are
oval shaped, more than 1 cm in diameter and heal
with scarring
3> Herpetiform ulcers - Recurrant crops of dozens of small ulcers which
are less than 0.5 cm are seen throughout oral mucasa
ACCORDING TO SEVERITY
1> Simple Apthasis - Recurrent attacks of any marphology with
distinct ulcer-free periods
2> Complex Apthasis - Almost constant presence of 3 or more than 3
oral ulcers
CLINICAL FEATURES
It usually occurs in 2nd and 3rd decades of Life.
Female predilection
It occurs most commonly in nonkeratinised mucasa such as buccal
and Labial mucasa tongue, soft palate, gingiva
Prodromal symptoms - prodromal burning itching for 24-48 hours,
before the ulcer appears.
The lesion is typically very painful, so it commonly interferes with
eating for several days
It begins as a single or multiple superficial erasions covered by gray
membrane.
It is surrounded by localised areas of erythema and develops within
hours.
Multiple lesions are present but the number and size varies.
The ulcers generally persist for 7-14 days, may recur after 1-4
months with no evidence of scarring.

MANAGEMENT
A> TOPICAL MANAGEMENT
Topical carticasteroid - 1% triamcinolone acetonide 3-4 times daily.
Other topical steroids are betamethasone syrup
and 0.01 % dexamethasone elixir.
Topical Anaesthetics - 2% viscous lidocaine, benzocaine and benzydamine
hydrochlaride may reduce pain.
Sucralfate - Topical application 4 times/day has shown soothing
effect on ulcer as it sticks to the mucus membrane.
Tetracycline mouthwash - 250 mg/30 ml used 4 times daily
Intralesional injection of sTeroid - Triamcinolone acten acetonide
Beclomethasone dipropionate spray
B> SYSTEMIC MANAGEMENT
Prednisolone tablet 20-30 mg/day Neways Fool
Betamethasone 2-3 mg/day for 4-8 days Tosiv
Systemic therapy for 15 days followed by intralesional injection
every 2 weeks for 2 months reduce the recurrence
Long term steroid therapy should be avoided

SIALOLITHIASIS
JESTID
to
Sialolithiasis is the formation of sialolith [salivary calculi] in the salivary
duct or gland resulting in the mechanical obstruction of the salivary flow.
RISK FACTORS
Dehydration - increases the viscasity and concentration of saliva
Reduced food intake / fasting / anarexia
sull
- decreases the demand for saliva, can bring about stasis of saliva
Certain drugs which Lower the production of saliva
- antihistamines, antihypertensives, diuretics ,antidepressants
Lack of saliva in certain conditions such as
- Sjogrens Syndrome, Xerastomia, tobacco smoking, hypercalcemia
TYPES
A> Ductal Sialolithiasis
B> Glandular Sialolithiasis
PREVALENCE
Submandibular ducts or glands - 80-94%
Paroted 5-18%
Sublingual n2%
The most common site is submandibular gland and Wharton's duct,
because its secretion is more alkaline, thicker, and the duct has a
tortuous course & smaller orifice
It usually occurs in 3rd-5th decades, with slight male predominance
Simultaneous siablithiasis in more than 1 salivary gland is rare
Bilateral occurance of the same gland is also rare
However, multiple sialoliths in the same gland or duct is common
CLINICAL FEATURES
Most common salivary gland disease in adults and the 2nd most common
salivary gland disease in children [ after mumps]
It is the major cause of unilateral, diffuse parotid or submandibular
gland swelling
Insome cases, the swelling is diffuse and resembles cellulitis.
Secondary infection often supervenes with fever.
occissociation
IN
with

Patients complain of pain and swelling during and after eating the
food when saliva production is at its peak. There is gradual
reduction of pain & swelling, which occurs at the resting period.
It recurs again repeatedly, when flow is stimulated.
Tenderness, trismus, decrease or absence of salivary flow, or
purulent discharge at the orifice of the duct may be present
Occasisionally, the sialolith presents no remarkable symptoms
INVESTIGATIONS
Radiograph
Sialography

Ultrasonography
MANAGEMENT
The treatment depends on the number, size and Location of sialolith.
If the sialolith is small and single, conservative management with
local heat, massage and sialogoques is attempted
In case of infection, initially antibiotics and anti-inflammatory kitaral
medication and enhanced use of sialogogues, salivary gland massage
and hyperhydration hoping for sportaneous passage is given
Techniques for removal of sialolith are -
A> Manual manipulation with the help of Lacrimal probes or dilators
[called as milking the gland ] - for sialoliths less than 2 cm, which
are Located peripherally near the ductal opening
B> Larger sialoliths in the anterior part are surgically removed,
by sialolithectomy, through a ductotomy, performed intraarally,
under LA. Posterior stones are removed under GA.

HERPES ZOSTER
Herpes zaster is an acute infectious viral disease of an extremely
painful and incapacitating nature, characterised by inflammation
of dorsal root ganglion or extramedullary cranial nerve ganglia,
associated with vescicular eruptions of skin and mucus membrane
of the area supplied by affected sensary nerve.
depriving
of ratural
It is also called as SHINGLES or ZONA
strength
The word zaster comes from the Latin for belt, as the lesion occurs in
belt- Like distribution
ETIOPATHOGENESIS
causative agent' - varicella zaster virus
Triggering factors are - trauma, development of malignancy,
Tumor involvement of dorsal root ganglia, local X-ray radiation
or immunosuppressive therapy
Herpes zaster is caused by reactivation of Latent V-Z virus,
which had been acquired during a previous attack of chickenpox
<01
CLINICAL FEATURES
Most common in adult Life
АИД (a
Although rare, it does occur in children under 15 years of age
Affects males and females with equal frequency
Initial symptoms are fever, malaise, pain and tenderness along the
course of involved sensory nerves, usually unilaterally.
In a few days the patient has a Linear papular or vescicular eruption
of the skin or mucasa supplied by the affected nerves.
A zone of erythema surrounds the vescicle, giving a dew drop on
a rose petal" appearance.
After the rupture of vescicles, healing commences by scabbing,
although secondary infection may intervene and slow the process
considerably.
Hutchinson sign - It is cutaneous zaster infection of the side of the
nase.

ORAL MANIFESTATIONS
Herpes zaster may involve the face by the infection of trigeminal nerve.
This usually consists of unilateral involvement of skin areas supplied by
either opthalmic, maxillary or mandibular nerves
Lesions of oral mucasa are extremely painful and may be found on the
palate, buccal mucasa, tongue, uvula, pharynx.
After several days of symptoms, intact vescicles appear which soon rupture
to leave areas of erasion of 1-5 mm with exforiation of teeth
Trigeminal herpes zaster occuring during tooth formation causes
pulpal necrasis and internal root resorption, exfoliation of teeth.
So, in the case of herpes zaster, several non-vital teeth can be seen.
DIAGNOSIS
Oral lesions can be easily identified by identifying characteristic
multinucleated giant cells & intranuclear inclusions [BIOPSY]
However this does not differentiate between herpes zaster & herpes
simplex.
This can only be done by fluorescent antibody staining Technique
or serological diagnosis.

HERPES SIMPLEX
Herpes simplex virus infections are common vescicular and ulcerative
lesions of mucasa and skin.
Most common intraoral herpetic lesions occur due to HSV-1 while HSV-2
primarily affects the genitals.
Herpetic to infections of oral cavity are of 2 types
A> Primary Herpetic Gingivastomatitis
B> Recurrent Herpetic Stomatitis /Herpes Labialis
HERPES LABIALIS
Recurrent Herpetic stomatitis is usually seen in adult patients and
manifests itself clinically as an attenuated form of primary disease.
PREDISPOSING FACTORS
Predisposing factors for reactivation include fever, upper respiratory
tract infection, Trauma and immunasuppression, fatigue, menstruation
and pregnancy, allergy or gastrointestinal disturbances.
PATHOGENESIS
Secondary herpes is the result of reactivation of Patent virus.
The viruses, once introduced in the body, appear to reside dormantly
within the regional ganglia, and when reactivation is Triggered, spread
along the nerves to the sites on oral thucasa and skin where they
destroy the epithelial cells and induce a typical nflammatory response.
CLINICAL FEATURES
The lesions are frequently preceded by a burning artingling sensation
and vescicles develop subsequently.
Recurrent intraoral herpetic Lesions almost invariably develop on the
oral mucosa that is tightly bound to periasteum such as palatal mucasa
and attached gingiva, especially in immuno-competent hast
In case of immunocompromised hast, the ulcers may OCCLIR bothat
Tightly bound and mobile mucasa
Recurrent Herpes Labialis - most Lesions appear on vermillion of Lip
and surrounding skin, they are gray or white vescicles which rupture
quickly leaving small red ulcerations, sometimes with slight erythe-
matous halo on covered by brownish crust on Lips.
Size ranges from 1-3 mm to 1-2 cT, rarely can be Large enough
to cause dissigurement.

DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

DIAGNOSIS
Past history of recurrent herpes Labialis. and a positive history of clase
contact with a patient with primary or recurrent herpes is helpful in
making diagnasis
Immanoflurescent staining of smears
Immunoperoxide technique
Serological tests such as complement fixation, RIA & ELISA
MANAGEMENT
5% acyclovir, 3% perciclovir and 10% docasanol are applied 4-6times
per day at the first prodrome. This will reduce the severity of lesion
Recurrent Herpetic Labialis can be suppressed by reducing trigger
factor such as by applying sunscreen at least 1/2 hour before sun
exposure.
Systematically acyclovir (400mg BD for 4 months) and valacyclovir
(500 mg- 1g OD for 4 -months) as preventive therapy has shown to
increase the time interval between 2 recurrent episodes.

Good oral hygiene. Proper hydration and electrolyte balance.

MUMPS
It is also called as epidemic parotitis.
Mumps is an acute contagious viral infection characterised by
unilateral or bilateral swelling of salivary glands usually the
parotid.
Other than salivary glands, it may also involve nerve, meninges,
pancreas, gonads.
ETIOLOGY
It is caused by RNA virus which belongs to family paramyxoviridae
and is found in saliva and urine
It spreads by droplet infection or direct contact
PATHOGENESIS
Once transmitted through respiratory droplet saliva, urine or
fomites, the incubation period is usually 2-3 weeks.
It starts replicating in the respiratory epithelium and spreads to
Local Lymph nodes, and subsequently develops viraemia
The affected area shows perivascular and interstitial mononuclear
cell infiltrates with oedema
Necrasis of acinar and epithelial duct cells is seen in salivary glands
and in the germinal epithelium of seminiferous tubules.
CLINICAL FEATURES
Predominant in childhood, more common in boys , age group 5-15 yrs
The disease is preceded by the onset of headache, chills, moderate
fever, vomitting and pain below the ear [ prodromal symptoms]

elevates the ear lasting for 1 week

Pain and Tenderness may be severe during the rapid phase of
parotid enlargement, the swelling reaches its maximum in about
3 days, tremains at its peak for 2-3 days and then subsides
gradually.
Most cases are unrecognised because of the absence of parotid swelling
The submandibular gland may also be involved separately or
in conjunction with the parotid gland.

ORAL SUBMUCUS FIBROSIS
OSMF is defined as an insidious chronic disease affecting any part
of the oral cavity and sometimes the pharynx.
It is always associated with juxta-epithelial inflammatory reaction
followed by fibroelastic changes of the Lamina propria with epithe-
Lial atrophy leading to stiffness of oral mucasa and causing trismus
and inability to speak.
The term OSMF was coined by Dr. Jashi in 1953.
EPIDEMIOLOGY
Most common precancerous lesion of Southeast Asian countries &
particularly Indian subcontinent.
ETIOLOGY
A> LOCAL FACTORS
Excessive areca nut chewing [single most important factor]
- Because it contains chemicals such as arecoline, arecaidine
which stimulates the fibroblast cells of Lamina propria &
produce excessive amount of collagen.
Excessive consumption of red chillies
- Because it contains capsaicin that produces hypersensivity
reactions in the oral mucasa

As the disease progresses, the oral mucasa becomes blanched and
slightly opaque and white fibrous bands appear
The density of fibrous deposit varies from a slightly whitish area
on the soft palate, causing no symptoms, to a dense fibrasis, causing
fixation or shartening or deviation of the uvula and soft palate
Gradual stiffening of oral mucasa with Loss of tissue resiliency,
fibrasis of pterygomandibular region often leads to trismus

experience referred pain to the ear.

It is a measurement instrument that tries to measure a characte-

from none to an extreme amount of pain.
METHOD
Ask the patient to indicate on the Line where the pain is in relation
to the 2 extremes.
Ask the patient to mark on the Line the point that he feels his
perception of his current state.
The VAS scare is determined by measuring in mm from the Left
hand end of the Line to the point that the patient marks.
When the VAS is properly explained to subjects, they can easily
understand its use and successfully use it to indicate his level
of pain response to hypersensitive stimuli.

higher

RISK FACTORS FOR ORAL CANCER
1> TOBACCO USE - Tobacco use is the major etiological factor for the
development of oral cancer.
The effect of tobacco use heavy alchohol consumption and poor diet
can probably explain 90% of cases of oral cancer.
Tobacco is used in 2 major farms
- smokeless tobacco
- smoking
Tobacco contains potent carcinogens Like nitrosamine, polycylic
hydrocarbons, polonium etc
10
Tobacco smoke contains CO, which is an important factor in the
development of oral cancer
2> ALCHOHOL - ALL forms of alchohol including hard Liquor, wine and
beer have been implicated in the etiology of oral cancer.
It acts by bits dehydrating effect on mucasa which increases the mucosal
permeability.
3> FAMILIAL AND GENETIC PREAISPOSITION - Though it is considered
as a risk factor, there is Little evidence supporting it.
4> ACTINIC RADIATION - It is a minor etiologic factor in case of lip
cancer
5> ORODENTAL FACTORS It is more prevalant in patients with
poor oral hygiene faulty restorations, sharp Teeth, ill-fitting
dentures and those with syphilitic glassitis
6> VIT. A DEFICIENCY - Vit. A deficiency can produce excessive
keratinisation of skin & HUCUS membrane and, thus may
predispase to carcinoma
7> IMMUNITY - The increasing incidence of oral cancers is clearly
age related, due to decreasing immune response with age.
It may occur in immunocompromised patients following
organ & bone marrow transplantation.
8> SYPHILIS - Oral malignancy is commonly found in Tertiary
stage of syphilis.
9> to IONISING RADIATION - Carcinoma of buccal mucosa may
occur as a complication of long term radiotherapy.

10> TRAUMA - Trauma in combination with other factors TOOD like миг chronic
check biting, denture use and irregularteeth may promote transforma-
-tion of epithelial cells.
11> VIRUS - Viruses such 0 as herpes simplex OF type 1, human papilloma
virus can be associated with oral cancer.

NEWER APPROACHES
a> Photodynamic Therapy -it involves selective uptake & retention
of preadministered photosensitizer in a tumor. It is followed
by irradiation with LASER of specific wavelength.
It leads to necrosis of tumor due to formation of singlet oxygen
b> Intralesional chemotherapy - in this, drug is injected directly
inthe tumor
c> Intra-arterial chemotherapy - drug is injected into articries
supplying the tumor
d> Intensity Modulated Radiation therapy - it delivers specified
dasages of radiation therapy directly to cancer cells. This will
spare the surrounding healthy tissue and also contributes to
reduced xerastomia & reduced myopathy.
e> gene Therapy - it involves introduction of functional genetic
material into target cells to replace defective gene.
